item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Source: Wikimedia Commons
Author: Silvio Tanaka
Licence: CC BY 2.0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this is a grossly underrated article. it does everything that it sets out to do and it does it all with just the right amount of style. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't get the last part, and the transcript is maybe a bit too lolrandom for my tastes, but the res is real well-written. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Previous downvote reversed. While the new ending is not perfect (it still hews very close to common site tropes), it is effective, and it gives a solid payoff to what remains a strong opening.

My new favourite part - when the red text warning reminds us that there is no Cliff Boggs, before anyone has suggested that there is!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yup, that's what I got from this. It's not that it didn't happen, it's that it isn't supposed to have happened. Up until that point, it was just a memetic infection. Then, suddenly, it was something altogether different.

…But then again, I've been playing with timeline alteration in music quite heavily thanks to On Mount Golgotha.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See, that's just the thing… there actually was a concert, but only for people affected by SCP-2971. For the ones who aren't infected, the concert didn't happen at all. It's like an alternate timeline slipping through into the current one.

Disavowment of the concert is necessary to prevent infection, so that's why it insists that it didn't happen, despite that it did (for those infected, who are in the alternate timeline).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is the first time I've read it completely, so I did not see the earlier Oneiroi reference. The pacing you've got now is terrific. In particular, I had a double-take at this:

This is a transcript of an audience recording of SCP-2971-A provided by a subject.

If there was no concert, there could be no recording, yet there is evidence of a recording. Therefore there is evidence of a concert.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Recent edits made this better. +1, will recommend others who downvoted/novoted reconsider their votes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It creeped me out so much I avoided even reading the last few lyrics. What the good god damn.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very much improved. I love it now.

item.title: Final edits are up.
item.content:encoded:
I have changed the ending and re-worked it a bit. It's not too drastically different from the previous version, but I do believe this version retains the mystery a lot better than the old one.

Thanks for the feedback, and I would encourage people to revise their votes if they like / don't like the new version.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I meant more on the level of, "end note implies that the universe has some kind of oddly bureaucratic system that controls realities and timelines".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wanted to clarify a bit on what I said earlier, about the effect seeping into the documentation. For me, the first note sets up an expectation, ala 2316, that this article would 'affect the reader'. A big red warning telling me that simpe knowledge of this information is Bad News Bears. Combined with this line:

SCP-2971 is passed to an uninfected subject through reading or discussing the topic of SCP-2971-A or its contents with an infected person.

Just reinforced that expectation. So after reding the interview, and with the established method of transmission in mind, I was expecting the rest of the article to screw with me. In-universe, that would be the ideal point for the reader to become 'infected' with information reaffirming the anomalous alt-reality (or whatever the hell it is).

I saw the version in your sandbox with the red countermeasures (countermemesures?), and I don't feel like adding them is the answer. For one, its unessesary in-universe before the interview log, and out-of-universe, it draws the format-screw too close to -2316.

As an aside, really hope you add the audio log you wrote.

item.title: Feedback-induced Changes.
item.content:encoded:
So after reading even more feedback after the last edits, the consensus seems to be that it is either

A: Great until the last bit sucks the mystery out of it, or

B: The ending is the hook, and the rest of it doesn't match. Apparently opinions are divided on the Protocol Myra parts.

I have re-written a lot of it, you can check it out in my sandbox here: http://scpsandbox2.wikidot.com/multiscoop. These edits are major enough to the concept of it that I don't want to just do it and hope it goes well. I want to get some opinions on it first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think you should drop the pre-article note. It made me think of the bodies in the water which I do not recognize, and that article did that gimmick better. Plus, it's not really the focus here: that's the final two addenda, and I think they make this work, on top of this being both a good meme and a good "real person" article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Retrobliterated

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would be a pretty solid conclusion to this article, in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What if the DMCA takedown resulted in the anomaly being neutralized?

"The memorystream in question was copied into your timeversuality in direct violation of the Untranslatably Redacted Act of 2944; all recordings and descriptions of the memorystream have been destrobliterated. If you believe this destrobliteration to have been done in error, please contact…"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know what. I dig it, partially. As a memetic anomaly I found this piece to be pretty solid example of an idea spreading itself into existence. I'm the biggest fan of the ending and the whole C&D note at the end, but the rest of the article kept me entertained enough to upvote.

+1. Think about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm another in this same boat. My thoughts throughout the early going went straight to 2316, and while on one level a repeat of that effect is a bit unnecessary, I actually thought this outdid the unrecognisable bodies in the water in some respects, by underplaying things to allow the tension to build.

But I was worried about the direction of the skip by the time I read about the man in the suit, and the final note was far too much like 2207, and undermined what was very good work up until then. It reinforced that this was a rehash of existing tropes, and deflated the tension entirely.

All that said, there is some really good writing in this, and I look forward eagerly to your next submissions.

item.title: Re: I've taken the new feedback into consideration, and I'll rework the ending a bit.
item.content:encoded:
It's a good attempt at fixing it, but I think at the heart of it, I just liked the strangeness and the mystery, and that being attached to some other plot narrative lessens it for me. This seems to be working out for you so you do what you will, but I still can't get behind it by the end.

EDIT: Also, was there a reason for removing the red repeated counter-messages? I really thought those added something to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hm. Major difference between this and 2207 is that 2207 is "we accept that you didn't know the consequences, so this is a friendly polite C&D notice, with a warning that if you don't C&D, we'll fucking obliterate you", whereas this is just an interdimensional copyright violation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seconding, yeah, I really want to like this but it just feels disappointing. The twist reminds me of 2207, but 2207's twist works on a level that this article does not reach.

It's difficult to diagnose why exactly one works while the other flops, it might be a result of the narrative familiarity: 2207's narrative is more basic but packs in enough detail to keep you along until the twist, whereas this article is more bizarre and then becomes a letdown when the twist isn't nearly as bizarre as you've expected.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Personally, I would've liked some of the memetic screwery to work its way into the documentation after the interview log. I was expecting the second and third addenda to play with the concept a bit more.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: so basically the Foundation was issued an extradimensional DMCA C&D which blanked their documentation for copyright reasons?

item.title: Re: I've taken the new feedback into consideration, and I'll rework the ending a bit.
item.content:encoded: I actually preferred the longer ending, where it was implied that there was some sort of rogue being causing it all. I feel like the shorter ending is less mysterious now.

item.title: I've taken the new feedback into consideration, and I'll rework the ending a bit.
item.content:encoded:
The concept of the D.P.C. is something I still feel belongs in this article: however, people have made it clear that they don't like Oneiroi being in this. I''m going to remove them and shorten the end note a bit so it's not so explainy.

Thanks for the comments, and I hope you re-evaluate your votes after you read the changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have similar opinions to Kalinin. The ending gets rid of all the mystery and doesn't really replace it with a strong payoff. I really wish I could jam to this, but it's not happening.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
These are my thoughts exactly. When I saw that bright red text, I immediately thought "THE 51ST STATE IS NOT A PLACE," "You do not recognize the bodies in the water."

Instead, it's mundane by those sorts of standards. This started out creepy but petered out and ended up relying on a note at the end. I feel like notes at the end should leave the reader wanting to know more, not wrap the skip up in a pretty bow as if to say, "And that's how I got to Memphis."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The phrase itself is not memetic,

Yes it is (or would be if the memetic model is valid); "think about it" is coherent information. I think you mean it isn't anomalous.

ETA: Looks like this has been fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really, really want to like this. The little reminders to the reader coupled with the subject material do a nice job conveying something that is outwardly prosaic and kind of strange, with undercurrents that something is very, very wrong. Great mood throughout.

Then the mystery and everything else gets sapped when we get a sort of half-hearted GoI explanation tacked on it. I can't downvote something that I enjoyed as much as I did about 80% throughout, but I can't upvote with that sour note at the end, either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was some really creepy and odd stuff that is right up my alley. The photo seems a bit low-res, but I don't mind. You could excuse it as a video recording I guess. This was really interesting regardless.

And then… I get it, this would be up Oneiroi's alley, but the fact that this kind of thing appeared out of nowhere, with only a man in a suit looking worried about the concert, that gets my gears rolling. Oneiroi sticking their weird dream parasitic fingers in it seems odd when they sound like any other random group or person on this site. Oneiroi's involvement is tenuous at best, and feels a bit weird. Even if Oneiroi did cause this contrary to the letter, I think SCP-2272 did this concept better.
